challenges.prompt: This external stylesheet is linked to a page. What visual result does this rule produce for every <h1> on the page?
options: Every <h1> appears in dark blue at 32 pixels, larger than the default black heading
Only the first <h1> on the page turns dark blue; the rest stay black
The whole page background turns dark blue and text becomes 32px
challenges.explanation: The selector h1 targets ALL <h1> elements, and the two declarations change their color and font-size. A tag selector applies to every matching element, not just the first, and it styles the elements themselves, not the page background.

challenges.prompt: This CSS rule is supposed to turn paragraphs red, but nothing changes. What's wrong?
options: A property and value must be separated by a colon (color: red;), not an equals sign
You cannot use the named colour 'red'; only hex codes work

The selector should be

with angle brackets, not p
challenges.explanation: Inside a CSS rule each declaration is written as property: value; — the property and value are joined by a colon and the declaration ends with a semicolon. An equals sign is not valid CSS syntax, so the browser ignores the broken declaration.

challenges.prompt: Complete the <link> tag that loads an external stylesheet named styles.css into the page's <head>. Fill in the attribute that points to the file.
challenges.explanation: An external stylesheet is attached with <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">. The href attribute holds the path to the CSS file. If the href path is wrong, the browser silently loads no styles and the page appears unstyled.

challenges.prompt: Fill in the missing character so this line is a valid CSS comment. Comments in CSS start with /* and end with these two characters.
challenges.codeTemplate:
/* This sets the brand colour {{blank}}
color: #4f46e5;
challenges.explanation: A CSS comment opens with /* and closes with */. Everything between the two markers is ignored by the browser. Forgetting the closing */ would accidentally comment out the code that follows, so the color declaration would never apply.
